content.full_text:
State of California

Fair Political Practices Commission
P.O. BOX 807 • SACRAMENTO, 95804 • • • 1100 K STREET BUILDING, SACRAMENTO, 95814

Technical Assistance • • Administration • • Executive/Legal • • Enforcement • • Statements of Economic Interest
(916) 322-5662          322-5660          322-5901          322-6441          322-6444

October 9, 1981

Mrs. Evelyn Reynolds
Salinas City Clerk
200 Lincoln Avenue
Salinas, CA 93901

A-81-10-119

Dear Mrs. Reynolds:

Thank you for your letter requesting advice from this
office. As we discussed, those questions you had involving
enforcement matters have been referred to Roger Brown of our
Enforcement Division.

The question I will address was that concerning possible
penalties. In a case of noncompliance, which was the situation
when your letter was written, you should read Government
Code Section 91000, regarding criminal sanctions; Section
91003, involving injunctions; and Sections 91004-91005,
discussing civil liability. In a late filing situation, as
this later became, you should look at Section 91013, which
provides for a $10 per day penalty for each day after the
deadline, until the report is filed. The amount of the
penalty may not exceed the cumulative amount stated in the
late report, which was, in this case, a semi-annual campaign
statement.

If you have any additional questions, please feel free
to call me at 916/322-5901.

Very truly yours,

Janis L. Shank
Counsel
Legal Division

JLS:km

City of Salinas
OFFICE OF THE CITY CLERK 200 Lincoln Avenue • Salinas, California 93901 • (408) 758-7381

August 18, 1981

Fair Political Practices Commission
1100 K Street
Sacramento, CA 95814

Gentlemen:

In connection with a General Municipal Election held on
June 2, 1981 in the City of Salinas, a candidate for a
City Council seat has not filed the semi-annual campaign
statement due on July 31, 1981. He also did not file
any threshold statements, but I have to assume he neither
received nor spent $500.00 or more. I sent reminder letters
to all candidates on July 14th and wrote a second letter
to this particular gentleman which was mailed after 5:00
p.m., Friday, July 31, 1981. When the mail received on
Monday, August 3, 1981, did not bring this gentlemen's
campaign statement, I telephoned him at his place of
business to remind him again. On either August 4th or
5th he came to my office to pick up duplicate forms and
I am told he made the comment that he guessed he would
have to use the long form; however, he was given both.
Not hearing from him again, I telephoned him on August
7th. On August 10th I wrote another letter and told
him he was being assessed $70.00 in penalties and an
additional $10.00 for each additional day he is delinquent. On August 14th I directed our Finance Office to
bill him $100.00 in penalties.

My questions are several:

1. Even though it is a candidate's responsibility
to comply with the law once he has been advised
of it, is there something else I should do?

(continued)

Fair Political Practices Commission
August 18, 1981
Page 2


2. The 1981 Information Manual on Campaign Disclosure
Provisions, page 3, refers to substantial civil
and criminal penalties, as well as late filing
penalties of $10.00 per day. What are these
additional penalties?

3. How do I enforce the $10.00 per day penalty if this
gentleman refuses to pay?

4. On the basis that this may be a "test" case, what
legal recourse do I have?

I have read the Government Code Sections pertaining to
these matters (and think I understand them), but I would
like a response in writing from your office prior to
taking any further actions. This has been discussed with
our City Attorney and he agrees that it would be wise to
have your written opinion on file.

Very truly yours,

[signature]
(Mrs.) Evelyn Reynolds
City Clerk of Salinas

sections.question: Even though it is a candidate's responsibility to comply with the law once he has been advised of it, is there something else I should do? The 1981 Information Manual on Campaign Disclosure Provisions, page 3, refers to substantial civil and criminal penalties, as well as late filing penalties of $10.00 per day. What are these additional penalties? How do I enforce the $10.00 per day penalty if this gentleman refuses to pay? On the basis that this may be a "test" case, what legal recourse do I have?
sections.conclusion: In a case of noncompliance, which was the situation when your letter was written, you should read Government Code Section 91000, regarding criminal sanctions; Section 91003, involving injunctions; and Sections 91004-91005, discussing civil liability. In a late filing situation, as this later became, you should look at Section 91013, which provides for a $10 per day penalty for each day after the deadline, until the report is filed. The amount of the penalty may not exceed the cumulative amount stated in the late report, which was, in this case, a semi-annual campaign statement.
sections.question_synthetic: What penalties apply to a candidate who fails to file a required semi-annual campaign statement, what are the civil and criminal penalties beyond the $10/day late filing penalty, how can the late filing penalty be enforced, and what legal recourse exists if this is a test case?
sections.conclusion_synthetic: The applicable penalties are found in Government Code Sections 91000 (criminal sanctions), 91003 (injunctions), 91004-91005 (civil liability), and 91013 (late filing penalties of $10/day capped at the amount of the late report). The FPPC referred enforcement matters to the Enforcement Division and directed the requester to specific statute sections for guidance.
sections.parsing_notes: No section headers found; LLM: Document contains both the incoming request (dated August 18, 1981) and the FPPC response letter (dated October 9, 1981). Response by Janis L. Shank, Counsel. No formal QUESTION/CONCLUSION headers but content clearly separates incoming request and FPPC response. Enforcement matters were referred to Roger Brown of Enforcement Division, so not all questions answered by responding counsel.

embedding.qa_text:
QUESTION: Even though it is a candidate's responsibility to comply with the law once he has been advised of it, is there something else I should do? The 1981 Information Manual on Campaign Disclosure Provisions, page 3, refers to substantial civil and criminal penalties, as well as late filing penalties of $10.00 per day. What are these additional penalties? How do I enforce the $10.00 per day penalty if this gentleman refuses to pay? On the basis that this may be a "test" case, what legal recourse do I have?

CONCLUSION: In a case of noncompliance, which was the situation when your letter was written, you should read Government Code Section 91000, regarding criminal sanctions; Section 91003, involving injunctions; and Sections 91004-91005, discussing civil liability. In a late filing situation, as this later became, you should look at Section 91013, which provides for a $10 per day penalty for each day after the deadline, until the report is filed. The amount of the penalty may not exceed the cumulative amount stated in the late report, which was, in this case, a semi-annual campaign statement.

embedding.summary: The City Clerk of Salinas requested written advice regarding penalties and enforcement procedures for a candidate's failure to file a semi-annual campaign statement by the July 31, 1981 deadline. The FPPC responded by citing the applicable Government Code sections governing criminal sanctions, injunctions, civil liability, and late filing penalties, while directing enforcement matters to the FPPC's Enforcement Division.
